title: Battle of Hastings and the Norman Conquest of England
summary: On Saturday 14 October 1066, on a low ridge in Sussex later called Battle, a Norman-French army under Duke William of Normandy destroyed the English fyrd of King Harold Godwinson. Harold died beneath his dragon standard; his housecarls fell around him; and within ten weeks a French-speaking dynasty was crowned in Westminster Abbey. The battle ended Anglo-Saxon rule in England, reoriented the island toward the continent, and set in motion a linguistic, legal, and architectural revolution whose traces still shape the English-speaking world.

sections.heading: Background

image.caption: The Battle of Hastings, engraved by W. Bromley after a painting by Philip James de Loutherbourg, 1804.
image.credit: Philip James de Loutherbourg / W. Bromley, via Wikimedia Commons

sections.body:
The crisis of 1066 grew out of a disputed succession that had been maturing for a generation. The childless English king Edward the Confessor, part Norman by upbringing after long exile at the court of his uncle Duke Robert I, died on 5 January 1066 after a reign of tangled loyalties between his native Wessex nobility and the Norman relatives of his mother Emma. The Witan, the council of English magnates, at once elected Earl Harold Godwinson, the most powerful noble in the kingdom and Edward's brother-in-law, and he was crowned in the newly consecrated Westminster Abbey the following day, the same day Edward was buried. Two rival claimants at once prepared for war, and the appearance of a bright comet in the spring, now known to have been Halley's, was read across Christendom as an omen of great upheaval.

Duke William of Normandy, a great-nephew of Emma through her brother Duke Richard II, insisted that Edward had promised him the throne in 1051 during a visit and that Harold himself, shipwrecked on the Norman coast around 1064, had sworn on hidden relics to uphold that claim. William's father-in-law Baldwin V of Flanders promised Flemish troops. Pope Alexander II, guided by his ambitious archdeacon Hildebrand, endorsed William's cause on the grounds of the alleged perjury and sent a consecrated banner and a papal ring. Through the summer William assembled a fleet of perhaps seven hundred ships at the mouth of the Dives and later moved it east to Saint-Valery-sur-Somme. His army was drawn from Normandy, Brittany, Flanders, Maine, and further afield, promised lands in an England that had not yet been conquered.

The second claimant was King Harald Hardrada of Norway, a giant of the northern world who as a young man had served in the Byzantine Varangian Guard. Joined by Harold Godwinson's exiled brother Tostig, whom the English king had driven from the earldom of Northumbria the previous year, he sailed with more than three hundred longships, defeated the northern earls Edwin and Morcar at Fulford Gate on 20 September, and occupied York. The English king had spent the summer waiting on the Isle of Wight for a Norman landing that adverse winds delayed; when his militia's compulsory term of service expired in early September he was forced to stand it down. On word of the Norwegian invasion he marched north at speed and on 25 September 1066 caught the Norwegian army unprepared at Stamford Bridge outside York. Harald Hardrada and Tostig were both killed; the Viking dead were so numerous that only two dozen ships were needed to carry the survivors home. It is often called the last Viking invasion of England.

Three days later, on 28 September, William's fleet crossed the Channel unopposed after weeks of unfavourable winds and landed at Pevensey on the Sussex coast. He fortified a beach-head at Hastings, using prefabricated wooden components he had brought over the Channel, and began systematically burning the surrounding villages, an old feudal method of forcing an adversary to fight and one that had the added value of striking at Harold's personal estates in Sussex. Harold, still north of the Humber, learned of the landing around 1 October, marched his exhausted household troops nearly three hundred kilometres south in about a fortnight, paused briefly in London to gather what levies he could, and pressed on to meet William, ignoring advice from his brother Gyrth to wait and let the invader come further inland. By the evening of 13 October the two armies were within sight of one another on the road from Hastings to London.

sections.heading: What happened

image.caption: The death of King Harold in Scene 57 of the Bayeux Tapestry: 'HAROLD REX INTERFECTUS EST'.
image.credit: Bayeux Tapestry (11th century); photo by Myrabella, via Wikimedia Commons

sections.body:
At dawn on Saturday 14 October 1066 Harold's army, perhaps seven to eight thousand strong, took up a defensive position on a ridge at Senlac, athwart the London road. The English formed a densely packed shield wall two-deep across the crest, the elite housecarls with their long two-handed Danish axes in the front rank and the fyrd militia behind. Their flanks were protected by boggy ground.

William deployed his army of similar size in three divisions: Bretons on the left, Normans in the centre under his own command, and French and Flemings on the right. Each division was arranged with archers in the first line, infantry in the second, and mounted knights behind. The battle began around nine in the morning with a shower of Norman arrows, most of which stuck harmlessly in English shields.

Wave after wave of Norman infantry and then heavy cavalry climbed the slope into a wall of axes, spears, and hurled javelins. The Breton wing broke and fled and the English right pursued them downhill, leaving the ridge and exposing themselves to a countercharge. William, whose horse was killed under him, tore off his helmet to prove he was still alive and rallied his men, who wheeled and cut the pursuers down. Twice more that afternoon the Normans staged feigned retreats and drew groups of Englishmen out of the shield wall to be destroyed in the open.

As the autumn light began to fail, William ordered his archers to shoot at a high angle, dropping arrows onto the heads of the tightly packed defenders. Norman infantry and cavalry pressed the exhausted line simultaneously. Late in the day Harold was struck down beside his personal standards, the Dragon of Wessex and the Fighting Man. The Bayeux Tapestry, embroidered within two decades of the battle, shows a figure clutching an arrow to his face and, beside him, another figure being cut down by a mounted knight; medieval and modern historians still debate which represents the king. His brothers Gyrth and Leofwine had already fallen. The shield wall dissolved into a rout in the twilight; a rearguard of housecarls made a last stand at a ravine known as the Malfosse. William slept that night on the battlefield among the dead.

sections.heading: Aftermath & consequences

image.caption: Tactical dispositions at the Battle of Hastings, 14 October 1066.

sections.body:
The victory did not immediately deliver the kingdom. William marched east and north, storming Dover, taking Canterbury, and encircling London. The remaining English magnates, including the young Edgar the Aetheling, submitted at Berkhamsted. On Christmas Day 1066 William was crowned in Westminster Abbey by Archbishop Ealdred of York. When the crowd inside the church shouted their acclamation, Norman guards outside mistook the noise for a riot, set fire to nearby houses, and sent the new king through his coronation in a half-empty, smoke-filled nave.

Rebellions rolled across the country for the next five years. The most serious, in the north in 1069 to 1070, was crushed by the notorious Harrying of the North, a scorched-earth campaign that destroyed villages, crops, and livestock from the Humber to the Tees and left the region so depopulated that the Domesday commissioners still recorded much of Yorkshire as waste sixteen years later. Anglo-Saxon earls were killed, exiled, or dispossessed; by around 1086 fewer than a dozen major English landholders survived. Their estates went to William's Norman, Breton, and Flemish followers.

A new class of stone castles, from the Tower of London to the vast bailey at Windsor, was thrown up across the kingdom to hold down conquered populations. The English Church was overhauled by Archbishop Lanfranc of Canterbury, who replaced most Anglo-Saxon bishops and abbots with continental reformers and rebuilt cathedrals on a Romanesque scale, at Durham, Winchester, and Ely. In 1085 to 1086 William commissioned the Domesday Book, a landholding survey of extraordinary detail that recorded who held what, and what it was worth, across most of the kingdom, and that remains one of the great administrative documents of medieval Europe.

sections.heading: Legacy
sections.body:
The most lasting legacy of Hastings was linguistic. Norman French became the language of the court, the law, and the higher clergy; Latin remained the language of scholarship and the church. English survived in the mouths of peasants and townsfolk, but during the next three centuries it absorbed thousands of French words, from beef and mutton on the table to justice, parliament, and government in public life. When English re-emerged as a written language in the fourteenth century it was a hybrid tongue that had lost much of its Anglo-Saxon inflection and gained a doubled vocabulary. Modern English is unimaginable without 1066.

Politically the conquest tied England's fate to France for four centuries. Because William and his heirs held Normandy as vassals of the French king but England in their own right, cross-Channel wars became almost continuous, from the loss of Normandy in 1204 to the Hundred Years' War of the fourteenth and fifteenth centuries. The Anglo-Norman monarchy also imported continental feudal institutions and a stronger central royal administration, which paradoxically laid the ground for the very English tradition of law and taxation later constrained by Magna Carta in 1215.

The battle itself became one of the most commemorated events of European history. William founded Battle Abbey on the spot where Harold had fallen, its high altar reputedly on the site of the English standard; the ruins still stand. The Bayeux Tapestry, seventy metres of embroidered wool now displayed in Bayeux, tells the story from a broadly Norman point of view but preserves an astonishing amount of everyday detail, from ship-building and cooking to the appearance of Halley's comet in the spring of 1066, taken by many at the time as an omen of the coming upheaval.

worldContext.text: Song China under Emperor Yingzong (r. 1063 to 1067), who died in January 1067 and was succeeded by his teenage son Shenzong, was in the middle of a century of extraordinary economic and technological growth. Movable-type printing had been invented by the artisan Bi Sheng only a generation earlier, around 1040, and the Song capital Kaifeng was probably the largest and richest city in the world, its population estimated at close to a million, with paper money (the jiaozi), coal-fired iron works producing more iron per capita than eighteenth-century Britain, gunpowder weapons, magnetic compasses, and a vast civil service recruited by written examination. The great landscape painter Fan Kuan had died a generation earlier; the polymath Shen Kuo would soon publish his Dream Pool Essays; and under Shenzong the statesman Wang Anshi would launch the celebrated New Policies. To the north the Khitan Liao and, from 1038, the Tangut Western Xia held the steppe frontier and extracted annual tribute from the Song court in what was, for the merchants at least, a period of unusual peace. In Japan the Fujiwara regents dominated the imperial court at Kyoto (Heian-kyo) in the last decades of the classical Heian age, whose court literature had recently produced Murasaki Shikibu's Tale of Genji.

image.caption: Fan Kuan (c. 960 to 1030), Travelers Among Mountains and Streams, hanging scroll, ink on silk, early eleventh century. National Palace Museum, Taipei — the great monument of Northern Song landscape painting.
image.credit: Fan Kuan, via Wikimedia Commons / Google Art Project

worldContext.text: The Seljuk Turkish sultan Alp Arslan (r. 1063 to 1072) was pushing westward across Armenia and Anatolia; his crushing defeat of the Byzantine emperor Romanos IV Diogenes at Manzikert on 26 August 1071 would open the Anatolian plateau to Turkish settlement, cost the Byzantines their manpower reserve, and set the stage for the First Crusade a generation later. Alp Arslan's Persian-speaking vizier Nizam al-Mulk was systematising the Seljuk administration and founding the network of Nizamiya madrasas at Baghdad, Nishapur, and Isfahan that would become the intellectual centres of Sunni Islam. Fatimid Cairo, ruled by the long-lived Ismaili caliph al-Mustansir (r. 1036 to 1094), remained a great intellectual and commercial hub of the Mediterranean Islamic world through its Al-Azhar mosque-university, though a severe famine known as the Great Calamity of Mustansir was gathering in Egypt through the late 1060s. The Abbasid caliph al-Qa'im in Baghdad had by now become a symbolic figurehead behind the Seljuk sultan and his Turkic army. Al-Andalus was fragmented into the Taifa kingdoms; the great Zaragozan poet-philosopher Ibn Gabirol and the Cordoban Ibn Hazm had recently died.

image.caption: The Fatimid mosque of al-Hakim in Cairo, begun by the caliph al-Aziz in 990 and completed by his son al-Hakim in 1013. Fatimid Cairo remained the great cultural capital of the Mediterranean Islamic world in the eleventh century.

worldContext.text: Northern India was a mosaic of Rajput kingdoms locked in shifting alliances and rivalries under dynasties like the Gahadavalas of Kannauj, the Chandellas of Bundelkhand (whose spectacular Khajuraho temples had recently been completed), the Paramaras of Malwa, and the Chalukyas of Kalyani. The Chola dynasty of the Tamil south under Virarajendra Chola (r. 1063 to 1070), grandson of the great Rajendra Chola I, was at the height of its naval power in the Bay of Bengal, projecting influence into Sri Lanka, Srivijaya in Sumatra, and the Malay peninsula, and dispatching diplomatic embassies to Song China; its recent maritime expedition against Srivijaya in 1025 remained the most ambitious blue-water campaign of pre-modern India. The Chola workshops of Thanjavur were producing the celebrated lost-wax bronzes of Shiva Nataraja and Parvati that now grace museums from Chennai to New York. Muslim raids from Ghazni had eased since the death of Mahmud in 1030 but would resume with the rise of the Ghurids in the twelfth century. In Kashmir Somadeva was compiling the Kathasaritsagara, the great ocean of story, while Bilhana would soon write his celebrated Vikramankadevacharita in praise of the western Chalukya king Vikramaditya VI.

image.caption: Shiva Nataraja, lost-wax cast bronze from Tamil Nadu, Chola dynasty, eleventh century. Dallas Museum of Art.

worldContext.text: In West Africa the Almoravid movement, born among the Sanhaja Berbers of the western Sahara under the theologian Abd Allah ibn Yasin and his military leaders Yahya ibn Umar and Abu Bakr ibn Umar, had by around 1054 captured the trans-Saharan trade hub of Awdaghost from the empire of Ghana. By 1069 to 1070, under Abu Bakr's cousin Yusuf ibn Tashfin, they had founded Marrakesh (or according to some chronologies seized an earlier settlement there), which became their capital and the base for their subsequent Andalusi expansion after the emir of Seville, al-Mu'tamid, called them in against the Christian advance of Alfonso VI of Castile-Leon (Toledo fell in 1085; the Almoravids answered at Sagrajas in 1086). Fatimid Egypt controlled the Red Sea trade with the Ethiopian highlands, where the recently emerged Zagwe dynasty was consolidating power at Roha (later renamed Lalibela) and preparing for the great rock-cut church programme of the twelfth and thirteenth centuries. Along the East African coast Swahili trading towns from Mogadishu to Kilwa were flourishing on Indian Ocean commerce in gold, ivory, and mangrove poles.

image.caption: The Almoravid Koubba (Qubba Ba'adiyn) in Marrakesh, a small domed pavilion for ritual ablutions and one of the few surviving pieces of Almoravid architecture in Morocco. The Almoravids founded Marrakesh in the 1060s.

worldContext.text: In Mesoamerica the Toltec city of Tula in central Mexico was the dominant power, its warrior aristocracy under the legendary Ce Acatl Topiltzin Quetzalcoatl (whose historical career, if any, belongs to the previous century) influencing distant cultures as far as Chichen Itza in northern Yucatan; the twin capitals shared the plumed serpent iconography, the reclining chacmool figures, and the colonnaded ceremonial hall. In the Andes the Wari and Tiwanaku states had disintegrated a generation earlier, and a patchwork of smaller polities occupied the highland valleys of what is now Peru and Bolivia; on the north coast the successor Chimu polity was consolidating at Chan Chan. In the American Southwest Chaco Canyon in what is now northwestern New Mexico was in the middle of its great pueblo-building era, with Pueblo Bonito, Chetro Ketl, and Pueblo del Arroyo rising as multi-storey ceremonial great houses linked by an extraordinary network of engineered roads reaching more than a hundred kilometres across the desert. In the Mississippi valley the ceremonial centre at Cahokia was beginning to consolidate; its Monk's Mound and the palisaded core of the site would rise decisively over the next century.

image.caption: The multi-storey great house of Pueblo Bonito at Chaco Canyon in northwestern New Mexico, ceremonial and administrative centre of the ancestral Puebloan Chacoan world in the eleventh century.

worldContext.text: Polynesian navigators had reached almost every habitable island of the eastern Pacific and were already settled on the Society Islands, the Marquesas, the Cook Islands, and on Rapa Nui, whose colonisers by this date had begun quarrying the volcanic tuff of Rano Raraku for the first ancestral moai figures (the great period of moai construction and platform-building on the island runs roughly from the eleventh to the sixteenth centuries). Migration to Aotearoa (New Zealand) still lay a century or two ahead, in the great southward wave of Polynesian voyaging that would carry the ancestors of the Maori to Aotearoa around the thirteenth century, but expert double-hulled canoe (waka hourua) construction and star-path navigation were already carrying settlers across thousands of miles of open ocean. In highland New Guinea long-established agricultural communities in the Wahgi valley and elsewhere practised the intensive taro, yam, sugarcane, and pandanus cultivation whose beginnings archaeologists at Kuk Swamp trace back nine thousand years. Aboriginal Australians maintained the managed-burning regimes and complex land-management traditions that shaped the continent's ecosystems, and continued rock-art traditions in Arnhem Land and the Kimberley.

image.caption: The fifteen restored moai of Ahu Tongariki on Rapa Nui, the largest ceremonial platform on the island; the great moai-carving tradition begins in this period.

figures.name: William, Duke of Normandy
figures.role: Norman leader whose victory made him William I of England, known thereafter as the Conqueror; reigned 1066 to 1087
figures.credit: Bayeux Tapestry, late 11th century, via Wikimedia Commons

figures.name: Harold II Godwinson
figures.role: Last crowned Anglo-Saxon king of England, killed at Hastings after reigning nine months

figures.name: Harald Hardrada
figures.role: King of Norway and rival claimant, killed at Stamford Bridge on 25 September 1066

figures.name: Odo of Bayeux
figures.role: Half-brother of William, warrior-bishop present at the battle, later patron of the Bayeux Tapestry

images.caption: Bishop Odo of Bayeux, half-brother of William, rallies the Norman knights at the Battle of Hastings, from the Bayeux Tapestry, late 11th century.
The death of King Harold at Hastings, from the Bayeux Tapestry: the inscription 'HAROLD REX INTERFECTUS EST' runs above a figure clutching an arrow and another falling to a mounted knight.
